edges.description: Loss and remodeling of the vascular bed interact with hypoxia and endothelial dysfunction to sustain high pulmonary vascular tone.
edges.source: Pulmonary Vascular Bed Injury and Remodeling
edges.target: Hypoxic Pulmonary Vasoconstriction and Endothelial Dysfunction

edges.description: Vasoconstriction, remodeling, and vascular-bed loss increase pulmonary arterial pressure and right ventricular afterload.
edges.source: Hypoxic Pulmonary Vasoconstriction and Endothelial Dysfunction
edges.target: Pulmonary Hypertension and Increased Right Ventricular Afterload

edges.description: Persistent pressure loading causes compensatory right ventricular hypertrophy, then dilation and right-sided failure in susceptible patients.
edges.source: Pulmonary Hypertension and Increased Right Ventricular Afterload
edges.target: Right Ventricular Hypertrophy, Dilatation, and Failure

nodes.description: Alveolar hypoxia and endothelial mediator imbalance cause pulmonary artery smooth-muscle constriction and remodeling. In COPD-associated cor pulmonale, nitric-oxide/prostacyclin loss and endothelin excess are described as contributors to vasoconstriction and proliferation.
nodes.id: Hypoxic Pulmonary Vasoconstriction and Endothelial Dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Pulmonary hypertension and chronic cor pulmonale in COPD.
evidence.snippet: Hypoxia and endothelial dysfunction play a central role in the development of pulmonary hypertension.
chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and subsequent pulmonary artery hypertension, leading to increased right ventricular work and stress.

nodes.description: Pulmonary hypertension is the hemodynamic bridge between chronic lung or pulmonary vascular disease and right ventricular remodeling. Elevated pulmonary vascular resistance increases the work required from the right ventricle.
nodes.id: Pulmonary Hypertension and Increased Right Ventricular Afterload

evidence.reference_title: Pulmonary hypertension and chronic cor pulmonale in COPD.
evidence.snippet: Cor pulmonale is a maladaptive response to pulmonary hypertension.
pulmonary artery hypertension, leading to increased right ventricular work and stress.

nodes.description: Chronic lung, chest-wall, ventilatory-drive, or pulmonary vascular disease narrows or destroys portions of the pulmonary vascular bed. This raises pulmonary vascular resistance and provides the proximal substrate for secondary pulmonary hypertension.
nodes.id: Pulmonary Vascular Bed Injury and Remodeling

evidence.snippet: Pathogenetic mechanisms involve hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, mechanical narrowing of vessels and obstruction of the pulmonary vascular bed

evidence.reference_title: Pulmonary hypertension and right heart dysfunction in chronic lung disease.
evidence.snippet: The pathophysiology of PH in CLD is multifactorial and includes hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, pulmonary vascular remodeling, small vessel destruction, and fibrosis.

nodes.description: The right ventricle initially remodels and hypertrophies in response to chronic pulmonary pressure load. With progression, dilation and right-sided heart failure can develop, producing systemic venous congestion and reduced exercise capacity.
nodes.id: Right Ventricular Hypertrophy, Dilatation, and Failure

evidence.snippet: Chronic cor pulmonale is defined as right-heart hypertrophy or right-ventricular dilatation and/or chronic right-heart failure

evidence.reference_title: Pulmonary hypertension and right heart dysfunction in chronic lung disease.
evidence.snippet: The effects of PH on the right ventricle (RV) range between early RV remodeling, hypertrophy, dilatation, and eventual failure
